analogy: Think of "Doing Sociology Research Methods" through the chapter's own opening images: 82 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY I INTROUDUCTION Have you ever wondered why a subject like sociology is called a social science? More than any other discipline, sociology deals with things that are already familiar to most people. All of us live in society, and we already know... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 82 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY, INTROUDUCTION, CHAPTER 5, DOING SOCIOLOGY : RESEARCH METHODS. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Doing Sociology Research Methods is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 82 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY I INTROUDUCTION Have you ever wondered why a subject like sociology is called a social science?

The text explains that more than any other discipline, sociology deals with things that are already familiar to most people.

The text explains that all of us live in society, and we already know a lot about the subject matter of sociology — social groups, institutions, norms, relationships and so on — through our own experience.

The text explains that it seems fair, then, to ask what makes the sociologist different from other members of society.

The text explains that why should s/he be called a social scientist?

The text explains that as with all scientific disciplines, the crucial element here is method, or the procedures through which knowledge is gathered.

The text explains that for in the final analysis, sociologists can claim to be different from lay persons not because of how much they know or what they know, but because of how they acquire their knowledge.

The text explains that this is one reason for the special importance of method in sociology.

The text explains that as you have seen in the previous chapters, sociology is deeply interested in the lived experience of people.

The text explains that for example, when studying social phenomena like friendship or religion or bargaining in markets, the sociologist wants to know not only what is observable by the bystander, but also the opinions and feelings of the people involved.

The text explains that sociologists try to adopt the point of view of people they study, to see the world through their eyes.

The text explains that what does friendship mean to people in different cultures?

The text explains that what does a religious person think he/she is doing when performing a particular ritual?

The text explains that how do shopkeeper and customer interpret each other’s words and gestures while bargaining for a better price?

The text explains that the answers to such questions are clearly part of the lived experience of actors involved, and they are of great interest to sociology.

The text explains that this need to understand both the outsider’s and the insider’s points of view is another reason why method is particularly important in sociology.

The text explains that methodological issues or questions are thus about the general problems of scientific knowledge-gathering that go beyond any one particular method, technique or procedure.

The text explains that for example, when a geologist studies rocks, or a botanist studies plants, they must be careful not to let their personal biases or preferences affect their work.

The text explains that however, the geologist and the botanist are not themselves part of the world they study, i.e. the natural world of rocks or of plants.

The text explains that because sociologists are also members of society, they will also have all the normal likes and dislikes that people have.

The text explains that even when the sociologist has no direct personal experience of the group s/he is studying, there is still the possibility of being affected by the values and prejudices of one’s own social context.

The text explains that part of the claims to superiority of research methods lies in the documentation of all procedures and the formal citing of all sources of evidence.

The text explains that this ensures that others can retrace the steps we have taken to arrive at a particular conclusion, and see for themselves if we are right.

The text explains that to deal with this possibility, sociologists explicitly mention those features of their own social background that might be relevant as a possible source of bias on the topic being researched.

The text explains that (You could go back to Chapter 1, and re-read the section (pp.

The text explains that 85 DOING SOCIOLOGY: RESEARCH METHODS means that competing and mutually incompatible schools of thought coexist within the discipline (Recall the discussion in Chapter 2 about conflicting theories of society).

The text explains that it means that objectivity has to be thought of as the goal of a continuous, ongoing process rather than an already achieved end result.

The text explains that of course, different methods are more or less suited to tackle different types of research questions.

The text explains that it is more important to ask if the method chosen is the appropriate one for answering the question that is being asked.

The text explains that the choice of method is usually dictated by the nature of the research question being addressed by the preferences of the researcher, and by the constraints of time and/or resources.

The text explains that this is sometimes referred to as ‘triangulation’, that is, a process of reiterating or pinpointing something from different directions.

The text explains that participant Observation Popular in sociology and specially social anthropology, participant observation refers to a particular method by which sociologist learns about society, culture and people that h/she is studying (Recall the discussion on sociology and social anthropology from Chapter 1).

The text explains that in these disciplines, scientists could not only work in the laboratory, they had to go out into ‘the field’ to learn about their subjects (like rocks, insects or plants).

The text explains that towards the end of the 19th and in the first decade of the 20th century many early anthropologists, some of whom were natural scientists by profession, began to carry out systematic surveys and first hand observation of tribal languages, customs, rituals and beliefs.

The text explains that this was agreed to — the Australian government even financed his trip and Malinowski spent a year and a half living in the Trobriand Islands.

The text explains that even before his Trobriand experience, Malinowski had been converted to the belief that the future of anthropology lay in direct and unmediated interaction between the anthropologist and the native culture.

The text explains that he was convinced that the discipline would not progress beyond the status of an intellectual hobby unless its practitioners engaged themselves in systematic first-hand observation preceded by intensive language learning.

The text explains that the use of interpreters was also to be avoided — it was only when the anthropologist could interact directly with the natives that a true and authentic account of their culture could be produced.

The text explains that the information obtained from one person would be cross-checked by asking other relatives the same questions, and after confirmation, a very detailed family tree could be drawn up.

The text explains that this exercise helped the social anthropologist to understand the kinship system of the community — what kinds of roles different relatives played in a person’s life and how these relations were maintained.

The text explains that a genealogy would help acquaint the anthropologist with the structure of the community and in a practical sense would enable him or her to meet with people and become familiar with the way the community lives.

The text explains that learning about these institutions and practices requires the anthropologist to ask endless questions about things that are taken for granted by members of the community.

The text explains that informants act as the anthropologist’s teachers and are crucially important actors in the whole process of anthropological research.

The text explains that for example, William Foote Whyte, an American sociologist, did his field work among members of a street ‘gang’ in an Italian-American slum in a large city and wrote a famous book Street Corner Society.

The text explains that he would like his book to be of some help to the people of the district; at least, he wants to take steps to minimise the chances of it doing any harm, fully recognising the possibility that certain individuals may suffer through the publication. — William Foote Whyte, Street Corner Society, p.342

The text explains that in the 1950s, many anthropologists and sociologists, both Indian and foreign began working on village life and society.

The text explains that what are the strengths and weaknesses of participant observation as a method?

The text explains that what are the basic elements of the survey method?

The text explains that describe some of the criteria involved in selecting a representative sample.

The text explains that state some of the weaknesses of the survey method.

The text explains that describe main features of the interview as a research method.

The text explains that basil Blackwell, Oxford University Press, New Delhi.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Introducing Sociology, Chapter 5: Doing Sociology Research Methods

prelims_traps: Do not treat 82 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY and INTROUDUCTION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Doing Sociology Research Methods; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Doing Sociology Research Methods through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 82 INTRODUCING SOCIOLOGY and INTROUDUCTION to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
